aiMessages.content:
Build a Telegram crypto price + alerts bot:

- scan new pair of solana listing on raydium
- Watchlist: add/remove coins and list watchlist.
- Price lookup: /price <symbol> (returns price + 24h change).
- Alerts: set above/below alerts per coin; list and remove alerts.
- Notifications: alert users when targets are hit.
- Reliability: caching, graceful errors, and “API down” fallback message.
- UX: quick inline buttons for common actions (Add alert, Remove, Watchlist).

Created the Solana Alert Watcher Telegram bot project with grammY, MongoDB persistence (with in-memory fallback), /start /help /price /watchlist /alerts /alert /reset, inline button flows, TTL caching, and two in-process background pollers (alerts + Raydium scanner) with backoff and production-safe logs.

planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Crypto Price + Alerts Bot (Solana/Raydium listings)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot built with Node.js (ESM) and grammY that helps users discover newly listed Solana pairs on Raydium, track prices, manage a personal watchlist, and receive above/below price alerts via Telegram notifications.

The bot prioritizes reliability (caching, timeouts, graceful degradation when upstream APIs are unavailable), good UX (inline buttons and guided flows), and operational simplicity (single Node.js service process with MongoDB for persistence). It will continuously scan Raydium for new listings, normalize tokens/pairs, and provide both user-driven lookups (/price) and event-driven notifications (new listings, alert triggers).

Product goals:
Primary user goals
1) Discover: Get notified when new Raydium pairs are listed (optionally filtered by criteria like liquidity, age, or quote token).
2) Track: Maintain a personal watchlist of tokens/pairs and quickly retrieve the latest price and 24h change.
3) Act: Set price alerts above/below targets per token and receive timely notifications when targets are crossed.

Product scope and assumptions
1) Token identification must be unambiguous. Symbols are not unique in crypto, so the bot should internally key on token mint address (Solana) and/or pair address, while allowing user-friendly symbol input with disambiguation.
2) Price and 24h change should come from a reliable price source. The bot should use at least one primary market data source and support a fallback source when the primary is degraded.
3) Raydium listing scanning should be robust against API changes and rate limits. The bot should avoid spamming users and provide configurable thresholds.

Success criteria
1) Alerts fire accurately with minimal false positives and no repeated spam.
2) Bot remains responsive even when upstream APIs fail (clear “API down” messages and cached values).
3) Operationally simple: single process, persistent state in MongoDB, and predictable background job behavior.

High-level architecture:
High-level architecture (single Node.js process)
1) Telegram interface layer: grammY bot handling commands, inline button callbacks, and conversational prompts.
2) Application services layer:
   1) TokenResolverService: resolves user input (symbol, address) to a canonical token record; handles disambiguation.
   2) MarketDataService: fetches price, 24h change, liquidity/volume where available; supports caching and fallback.
   3) WatchlistService: CRUD for watchlist items per user.
   4) AlertsService: CRUD for alerts, evaluation logic, deduplication, and notification formatting.
   5) RaydiumScannerService: periodically scans for new pairs, normalizes them, and emits “new listing” events.
   6) NotificationService: sends messages to users; central place for rate limiting, retries, and formatting.
3) Persistence layer (MongoDB): users, watchlists, alerts, token metadata, pair metadata, scanner cursors/checkpoints, and notification logs.
4) In-memory caching: short-lived caches for market data, token resolution results, and API health status.

Background scheduling within the single process
1) Raydium scan loop: runs on an interval, uses a cursor/checkpoint to only process new pairs.
2) Alert evaluation loop: runs frequently, pulls active alerts, batches by token, fetches prices once per token per cycle, evaluates conditions, and triggers notifications.
3) Optional cleanup loop: expires old logs, stale cache entries, and prunes resolved-but-unused token aliases.

Resilience patterns
1) Timeouts and circuit breaker behavior per upstream API: after repeated failures, mark API as degraded for a cool-down period.
2) Stale-while-revalidate caching: serve last known price for a short window with a disclaimer if fresh fetch fails.
3) Idempotent notifications: store alert trigger state so the same crossing does not repeatedly notify.

Operational boundaries
1) No external queues: all background work occurs in-process. MongoDB is the source of truth for state and cursor checkpoints.
2) Rate limiting: apply per-user command throttles and global API call throttles to prevent abuse and upstream bans.

Data model:
Core entities (MongoDB collections)
1) users
   1) telegramUserId (unique)
   2) chatId (for notifications; usually equals telegramUserId for private chats)
   3) createdAt, updatedAt
   4) settings
      1) quoteCurrency (e.g., USD)
      2) newListingsEnabled (boolean)
      3) newListingsFilters (minLiquidity, quoteToken, blacklists)
      4) timezone/locale (optional)

2) tokens
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) mintAddress (unique per chain)
   3) symbol, name, decimals
   4) logoUrl (optional)
   5) knownAliases (array of symbols/names for search)
   6) lastResolvedAt

3) pairs
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) dex ("raydium")
   3) pairAddress (unique)
   4) baseMintAddress, quoteMintAddress
   5) listedAt (first seen time)
   6) metricsSnapshot (liquidity, volume, price if available at discovery time)
   7) discoverySourceMetadata (raw identifiers needed for debugging)

4) watchlists
   1) telegramUserId
   2) items (or separate collection watchlistitems)
      1) type ("token" or "pair")
      2) mintAddress and/or pairAddress
      3) addedAt
   Notes: Using a separate watchlistitems collection scales better and simplifies queries.

5) alerts
   1) telegramUserId
   2) chain
   3) mintAddress (or pairAddress if you want pair-specific pricing)
   4) direction ("above" | "below")
   5) targetPrice (in user quote currency)
   6) status ("active" | "paused" | "triggered" | "deleted")
   7) createdAt, updatedAt
   8) lastEvaluatedAt
   9) lastNotifiedAt
   10) triggerState
      1) lastPriceSeen
      2) armed (boolean) to prevent repeated triggers without recross logic

6) raydiumscanstate
   1) key (e.g., "raydiumpairs")
   2) cursor (block/slot/time/page token depending on API)
   3) lastScanAt
   4) lastSuccessAt
   5) consecutiveFailures

7) notificationlog (optional but recommended)
   1) telegramUserId
   2) type ("alerttrigger" | "newlisting")
   3) entityId (alertId or pairAddress)
   4) sentAt
   5) messageHash or dedupeKey
   Purpose: diagnose delivery issues and prevent duplicates.

Indexes
1) alerts: (status, chain, mintAddress) and (telegramUserId, status)
2) watchlistitems: (telegramUserId) and (telegramUserId, mintAddress)
3) tokens: (chain, mintAddress) unique; text-like search over symbol/name/aliases via normalized fields
4) pairs: (dex, listedAt) and (pairAddress) unique
5) raydiumscanstate: key unique

Key flows:
Command and UX principles
1) Keep commands fast and deterministic; avoid multi-step unless needed.
2) Prefer inline buttons for common actions after a /price response: Add to watchlist, Set alert above, Set alert below, View watchlist, Remove.
3) Use disambiguation when input is ambiguous: show a short inline list of matching tokens (symbol + name + partial mint).

Flow: /start
1) Register user (upsert in MongoDB) and store chatId.
2) Show a compact menu with inline buttons:
   1) Price lookup help
   2) Watchlist
   3) Alerts
   4) New listings settings

Flow: /price <symbolOrAddress>
1) Resolve input via TokenResolverService:
   1) If exact mint address: map to token.
   2) If symbol: search known tokens; if multiple results, prompt with inline disambiguation.
2) Fetch market data via MarketDataService:
   1) Use cached quote if fresh.
   2) If stale, attempt refresh with timeout.
   3) If API fails, return last known price with “data may be delayed” note; if no cache, return “API temporarily unavailable”.
3) Respond with:
   1) Current price
   2) 24h change (percentage and direction)
   3) Last updated timestamp
   4) Optional: liquidity/volume if available
4) Provide inline buttons:
   1) Add to watchlist
   2) Set alert above
   3) Set alert below
   4) View alerts for this token

Flow: Watchlist management
1) /watchlist
   1) Show watchlist items with current price snapshot (best-effort; batch fetch with caching).
   2) For each item, provide inline actions: Remove, Set alert above/below, Refresh.
2) /watchlistadd <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then add item if not present.
3) /watchlistremove <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then remove.

Flow: Alerts management
1) /alert <symbolOrAddress> <above|below> <targetPrice>
   1) Resolve token.
   2) Validate targetPrice against sane bounds.
   3) Create alert as active.
   4) Reply with summary and inline actions: Pause, Delete, Add opposite-direction alert.
2) /alerts
   1) List active alerts grouped by token; show target, direction, last evaluated, and status.
   2) Inline actions per alert: Pause/Resume, Delete.
3) /alertremove <alertId>
   1) Soft-delete or mark deleted.

Flow: Alert evaluation and notification
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Query active alerts; batch them by mintAddress.
3) For each token batch:
   1) Fetch current price once.
   2) Evaluate all alerts:
      1) Above: trigger if price >= target and alert is armed.
      2) Below: trigger if price <= target and alert is armed.
      3) Re-arming policy: optionally re-arm when price crosses back over/under a configurable hysteresis band to avoid rapid flapping.
   3) On trigger:
      1) Record trigger in alert (status triggered or keep active but disarmed).
      2) Send message with price, target, and quick actions (Delete, Set new target, View chart link if you support external links).
      3) Write notificationlog entry to dedupe.

Flow: Raydium new pair scanning
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Fetch list of recent pairs since cursor (or lastScanAt).
3) Normalize pairs:
   1) Identify base/quote mints.
   2) Store pair if new.
   3) Enrich token metadata if missing.
4) Determine which users should be notified:
   1) Users who enabled new listings.
   2) Apply filters: quote token, min liquidity, blacklist.
5) Send a compact “new listing” notification with inline actions:
   1) Price
   2) Add to watchlist
   3) Set alert

Flow: “API down” fallback behavior
1) Maintain API health state in memory (and optionally persisted counters).
2) If market data API is degraded:
   1) Prefer cached responses for /price and watchlist.
   2) Alert evaluation loop can either pause (safer) or run on cached prices with strong disclaimers; recommended: pause and notify admins via logs.
3) All user-facing errors should be short, actionable, and not expose stack traces.

Flow: Inline callback handling
1) Inline button payloads should include minimal identifiers (token mint, alertId).
2) Validate ownership: ensure the telegramUserId matches the entity being modified.
3) Always acknowledge callback quickly and then edit message or send a follow-up for longer operations.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, command routing, and inline keyboards.

Persistence
1) MongoDB (already integrated) as the primary datastore for users, tokens, pairs, watchlists, alerts, scan cursors, and notification logs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caching (market data, token resolution results, API health) to reduce latency and upstream calls.

External integrations
1) Raydium pair discovery endpoint(s) for detecting new listings.
2) Market data provider(s) for spot price and 24h change. Use at least one primary and one fallback source.
3) Optional: object storage or simple filesystem storage if you later add token logos, cached metadata, or exported reports (not required for MVP).

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) Other env vars: MongoDB connection string, polling/webhook mode selection, scan intervals, rate limits, feature flags.

Deployment
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single service process.
2) Either long polling or webhook mode depending on environment; both supported by grammY. Webhook preferred if you have stable HTTPS ingress, polling acceptable for simplicity.

Observability
1) Structured logging (request context: update id, user id, command).
2) Basic metrics counters tracked in memory and emitted to logs (API failures, alert triggers, scan cycle durations).
3) Error reporting via logs; optionally persist critical failures and last-success timestamps in MongoDB for visibility.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Data consistency: use canonical identifiers (mint/pair addresses) internally; never rely solely on symbol.
2) Idempotency: notificationlog and alert triggerState prevent duplicate alerts during retries or restarts.
3) Safe restarts: background loops read their state from MongoDB (scan cursor, active alerts) so the service can restart without losing progress.

Performance and rate limiting
1) Batch market data requests by token for watchlist and alert evaluation to minimize API calls.
2) Use caching with short TTLs (e.g., 5–30 seconds for price depending on rate limits) and separate longer TTLs for token metadata.
3) Enforce per-user command rate limits (e.g., bursts) and global upstream API rate limits.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Validate and sanitize all user inputs (numbers, symbols, addresses).
2) Ownership checks for any mutation via callbacks or commands.
3) Avoid leaking internal IDs; if you expose alertId, use short opaque IDs or MongoDB ObjectId but ensure access checks.

User experience
1) Fast responses: prefer cached prices; if fresh fetch is slow, reply with cached value and a “refreshing” follow-up only if needed.
2) Clear messaging: show timestamp of price data.
3) Inline buttons for common follow-ups to reduce typing.

Operations
1) Health signals: store lastSuccessfulScanAt and lastSuccessfulMarketFetchAt; log warnings when stale.
2) Backoff strategies: exponential backoff on failing APIs; do not hammer upstream.
3) MongoDB indexes and cleanup: TTL-like cleanup via a periodic job for old notification logs if needed.

Testing strategy (design-level)
1) Contract tests for market data and Raydium endpoints using recorded responses.
2) Simulated alert evaluation with deterministic price sequences to verify crossing logic and re-arming.
3) Load simulation for many alerts to validate batching and latency.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initialize the bot and show the main menu with quick actions. (usage: /start) [Getting started]
• /help — Show usage instructions and examples for common tasks (price, watchlist, alerts, listings). (usage: /help) [Getting started]
• /price — Lookup current price and 24h change for a token by symbol or mint address. (usage: /price <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Prices]
• /watchlist — Show your watchlist with quick actions (remove, set alerts, refresh). (usage: /watchlist) [Watchlist]
• /watchlistadd — Add a token to your watchlist by symbol or mint address. (usage: /watchlistadd <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Watchlist]
• /watchlistremove — Remove a token from your watchlist by symbol or mint address. (usage: /watchlistremove <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Watchlist]
• /alert — Create a price alert for a token above or below a target price. (usage: /alert <symbolOrMintAddress> <above|below> <targetPrice>) [Alerts]
• /alerts — List your alerts and manage them with inline actions (pause/resume/delete). (usage: /alerts) [Alerts]
• /alertremove — Remove an alert by its identifier (also available via inline buttons). (usage: /alertremove <alertId>) [Alerts]
• /listings — Show the most recently discovered Raydium listings (with filters applied if configured). (usage: /listings) [Discovery]
• /listingson — Enable new Raydium listing notifications. (usage: /listingson) [Discovery]
• /listingsoff — Disable new Raydium listing notifications. (usage: /listingsoff) [Discovery]
• /settings — View and adjust settings such as quote currency and listing filters. (usage: /settings) [Settings]

2) Either long polling or webhook mode depending on environment; both supported by grammY. Webhook preferred if you have stable HTTPS ingress, polling acceptable for simplicity.

Observability
1) Structured logging (request context: update id, user id, command).
2) Basic metrics counters tracked in memory and emitted to logs (API failures, alert triggers, scan cycle durations).
3) Error reporting via logs; optionally persist critical failures and last-success timestamps in MongoDB for visibility.

Reliability and correctness
1) Data consistency: use canonical identifiers (mint/pair addresses) internally; never rely solely on symbol.
2) Idempotency: notificationlog and alert triggerState prevent duplicate alerts during retries or restarts.
3) Safe restarts: background loops read their state from MongoDB (scan cursor, active alerts) so the service can restart without losing progress.

Performance and rate limiting
1) Batch market data requests by token for watchlist and alert evaluation to minimize API calls.
2) Use caching with short TTLs (e.g., 5–30 seconds for price depending on rate limits) and separate longer TTLs for token metadata.
3) Enforce per-user command rate limits (e.g., bursts) and global upstream API rate limits.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Validate and sanitize all user inputs (numbers, symbols, addresses).
2) Ownership checks for any mutation via callbacks or commands.
3) Avoid leaking internal IDs; if you expose alertId, use short opaque IDs or MongoDB ObjectId but ensure access checks.

User experience
1) Fast responses: prefer cached prices; if fresh fetch is slow, reply with cached value and a “refreshing” follow-up only if needed.
2) Clear messaging: show timestamp of price data.
3) Inline buttons for common follow-ups to reduce typing.

Operations
1) Health signals: store lastSuccessfulScanAt and lastSuccessfulMarketFetchAt; log warnings when stale.
2) Backoff strategies: exponential backoff on failing APIs; do not hammer upstream.
3) MongoDB indexes and cleanup: TTL-like cleanup via a periodic job for old notification logs if needed.

Testing strategy (design-level)
1) Contract tests for market data and Raydium endpoints using recorded responses.
2) Simulated alert evaluation with deterministic price sequences to verify crossing logic and re-arming.
3) Load simulation for many alerts to validate batching and latency.

DOCS.md.content:
Solana Alert Watcher

What this bot does
It helps you look up Solana token prices, keep a personal watchlist, set one-time price alerts (above or below), and optionally get notified when new Raydium pairs are discovered.

Public commands
1) /start
Shows a welcome message and a menu.

2) /help
Shows usage examples and explains behavior when external APIs are unavailable.

3) /price <symbol|mint>
Looks up a token and shows its price in USD.
Examples:
/price SOL
/price So11111111111111111111111111111111111111112
Notes:
If you use a symbol and it matches multiple tokens, the bot may ask you to pick one by mint.

4) /watchlist
Opens the watchlist menu.
Subcommands:
/watchlist list
/watchlist add <symbol|mint>
/watchlist remove <symbol|mint>

5) /alerts
Opens the alerts menu and lists current alerts.

6) /alert <symbol|mint> above <price>
Creates an above alert.
Example:
/alert SOL above 250

7) /alert <symbol|mint> below <price>
Creates a below alert.
Example:
/alert SOL below 100

8) /reset
Clears your watchlist and alerts for this bot.

Inline buttons
After a /price lookup, you will see buttons like:
- Add to Watchlist
- Set Alert Above
- Set Alert Below
- Refresh

New pairs notifications
New Raydium pair notifications are opt-in.
Use the New Pairs menu from /start to enable or disable.

Environment variables
Required
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Your Telegram bot token.

Optional (recommended)
2) MONGODB_URI
MongoDB connection string. When set, your watchlist, alerts, and settings are persisted.
If missing, the bot still runs but data is stored in memory and is lost on restart.

Optional
3) PRICE_API_BASE_URL
Override the price API base URL. If not set, the bot uses a safe default.

4) RAYDIUM_API_BASE_URL
Override the Raydium API base URL. If not set, the bot uses a safe default.

5) CACHE_TTL_SECONDS
TTL for in-memory caches. Default is used if missing.

6) ALERT_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS
Alert scanner interval. Default is used if missing.

7) RAYDIUM_SCAN_INTERVAL_SECONDS
Raydium new pairs scanner interval. Default is used if missing.

Troubleshooting
If a price or Raydium call fails, the bot will say:
“Price data temporarily unavailable (API down). Please try again in a minute.”
It will keep running and retry later.

README.md.content:
Solana Alert Watcher

This is a Telegram bot (grammY, Node.js ES modules) that provides Solana token price lookup, per-user watchlists, per-user price alerts (above/below), and background scanning of newly listed Raydium pairs.

Features
1) /price lookup by symbol or mint
2) Watchlist add/remove/list (per user)
3) Alerts above/below (per user, trigger once)
4) Raydium new pair scanning (background) with opt-in notifications
5) Caching, timeouts, and friendly “API temporarily unavailable” messaging
6) MongoDB persistence (watchlists, alerts, users, Raydium state)

Architecture overview
1) src/index.js
   Starts the bot with long polling via @grammyjs/runner, clears webhook, and starts background pollers.
2) src/bot.js
   Creates the grammY bot, registers commands first, then callback handlers.
3) src/commands/*
   /start, /help, /price, /watchlist, /alerts, /alert, /reset
4) src/services/*
   priceService (token resolution + price), raydiumService (new pairs scan)
5) src/pollers/*
   alertPoller, raydiumScanner (single-process loops)
6) src/lib/*
   config, db, caches, logging, helpers

Setup
1) Install
   npm run install:root

2) Configure env
   Copy .env.sample to .env and set:
   - TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
   - MONGODB_URI (optional but strongly recommended; required for persistence)

3) Run locally
   npm run dev

4) Run production
   npm start

Commands (quick)
1) /start
2) /help
3) /price SOL
4) /price <mint>
5) /watchlist
6) /watchlist add SOL
7) /watchlist remove <mint>
8) /watchlist list
9) /alerts
10) /alert SOL above 250
11) /alert SOL below 100
12) /reset (clears your stored conversation memory collection if you had it; this bot uses /reset to clear watchlist+alerts state for safety)

Integrations
1) Price API
   - Configurable via PRICE_API_BASE_URL (optional)
   - Default fallback uses Jupiter public APIs
2) Raydium API
   - Configurable via RAYDIUM_API_BASE_URL (optional)
   - Default fallback uses Raydium public APIs

Database
Collections used
1) users
2) watchlists
3) alerts
4) raydium_state
5) raydium_pairs

Indexes are created on boot when MongoDB is configured.

Deployment notes (Render)
1) Use a Background Worker or Web Service. Long polling does not require an inbound port.
2) Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
3) Set MONGODB_URI to enable persistence.

Troubleshooting
1) Bot not starting
   - Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set
   - Check logs for "TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN set: true"
2) Price data unavailable
   - Upstream APIs can be down or rate-limited. The bot will return a friendly message and keep running.
3) Duplicate instance conflict (409)
   - Render deploy overlaps can cause 409 conflicts. This bot retries polling with backoff.

Extensibility
Add new commands under src/commands and they will be auto-registered via src/commands/loader.js. Keep /help output in sync.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Crypto Price + Alerts Bot (Solana/Raydium listings)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot built with Node.js (ESM) and grammY that helps users discover newly listed Solana pairs on Raydium, track prices, manage a personal watchlist, and receive above/below price alerts via Telegram notifications.

The bot prioritizes reliability (caching, timeouts, graceful degradation when upstream APIs are unavailable), good UX (inline buttons and guided flows), and operational simplicity (single Node.js service process with MongoDB for persistence). It will continuously scan Raydium for new listings, normalize tokens/pairs, and provide both user-driven lookups (/price) and event-driven notifications (new listings, alert triggers).

Product goals:
Primary user goals
1) Discover: Get notified when new Raydium pairs are listed (optionally filtered by criteria like liquidity, age, or quote token).
2) Track: Maintain a personal watchlist of tokens/pairs and quickly retrieve the latest price and 24h change.
3) Act: Set price alerts above/below targets per token and receive timely notifications when targets are crossed.

Product scope and assumptions
1) Token identification must be unambiguous. Symbols are not unique in crypto, so the bot should internally key on token mint address (Solana) and/or pair address, while allowing user-friendly symbol input with disambiguation.
2) Price and 24h change should come from a reliable price source. The bot should use at least one primary market data source and support a fallback source when the primary is degraded.
3) Raydium listing scanning should be robust against API changes and rate limits. The bot should avoid spamming users and provide configurable thresholds.

Success criteria
1) Alerts fire accurately with minimal false positives and no repeated spam.
2) Bot remains responsive even when upstream APIs fail (clear “API down” messages and cached values).
3) Operationally simple: single process, persistent state in MongoDB, and predictable background job behavior.

High-level architecture:
High-level architecture (single Node.js process)
1) Telegram interface layer: grammY bot handling commands, inline button callbacks, and conversational prompts.
2) Application services layer:
   1) TokenResolverService: resolves user input (symbol, address) to a canonical token record; handles disambiguation.
   2) MarketDataService: fetches price, 24h change, liquidity/volume where available; supports caching and fallback.
   3) WatchlistService: CRUD for watchlist items per user.
   4) AlertsService: CRUD for alerts, evaluation logic, deduplication, and notification formatting.
   5) RaydiumScannerService: periodically scans for new pairs, normalizes them, and emits “new listing” events.
   6) NotificationService: sends messages to users; central place for rate limiting, retries, and formatting.
3) Persistence layer (MongoDB): users, watchlists, alerts, token metadata, pair metadata, scanner cursors/checkpoints, and notification logs.
4) In-memory caching: short-lived caches for market data, token resolution results, and API health status.

Background scheduling within the single process
1) Raydium scan loop: runs on an interval, uses a cursor/checkpoint to only process new pairs.
2) Alert evaluation loop: runs frequently, pulls active alerts, batches by token, fetches prices once per token per cycle, evaluates conditions, and triggers notifications.
3) Optional cleanup loop: expires old logs, stale cache entries, and prunes resolved-but-unused token aliases.

Resilience patterns
1) Timeouts and circuit breaker behavior per upstream API: after repeated failures, mark API as degraded for a cool-down period.
2) Stale-while-revalidate caching: serve last known price for a short window with a disclaimer if fresh fetch fails.
3) Idempotent notifications: store alert trigger state so the same crossing does not repeatedly notify.

Operational boundaries
1) No external queues: all background work occurs in-process. MongoDB is the source of truth for state and cursor checkpoints.
2) Rate limiting: apply per-user command throttles and global API call throttles to prevent abuse and upstream bans.

Data model:
Core entities (MongoDB collections)
1) users
   1) telegramUserId (unique)
   2) chatId (for notifications; usually equals telegramUserId for private chats)
   3) createdAt, updatedAt
   4) settings
      1) quoteCurrency (e.g., USD)
      2) newListingsEnabled (boolean)
      3) newListingsFilters (minLiquidity, quoteToken, blacklists)
      4) timezone/locale (optional)

2) tokens
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) mintAddress (unique per chain)
   3) symbol, name, decimals
   4) logoUrl (optional)
   5) knownAliases (array of symbols/names for search)
   6) lastResolvedAt

3) pairs
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) dex ("raydium")
   3) pairAddress (unique)
   4) baseMintAddress, quoteMintAddress
   5) listedAt (first seen time)
   6) metricsSnapshot (liquidity, volume, price if available at discovery time)
   7) discoverySourceMetadata (raw identifiers needed for debugging)

4) watchlists
   1) telegramUserId
   2) items (or separate collection watchlistitems)
      1) type ("token" or "pair")
      2) mintAddress and/or pairAddress
      3) addedAt
   Notes: Using a separate watchlistitems collection scales better and simplifies queries.

5) alerts
   1) telegramUserId
   2) chain
   3) mintAddress (or pairAddress if you want pair-specific pricing)
   4) direction ("above" | "below")
   5) targetPrice (in user quote currency)
   6) status ("active" | "paused" | "triggered" | "deleted")
   7) createdAt, updatedAt
   8) lastEvaluatedAt
   9) lastNotifiedAt
   10) triggerState
      1) lastPriceSeen
      2) armed (boolean) to prevent repeated triggers without recross logic

6) raydiumscanstate
   1) key (e.g., "raydiumpairs")
   2) cursor (block/slot/time/page token depending on API)
   3) lastScanAt
   4) lastSuccessAt
   5) consecutiveFailures

7) notificationlog (optional but recommended)
   1) telegramUserId
   2) type ("alerttrigger" | "newlisting")
   3) entityId (alertId or pairAddress)
   4) sentAt
   5) messageHash or dedupeKey
   Purpose: diagnose delivery issues and prevent duplicates.

Indexes
1) alerts: (status, chain, mintAddress) and (telegramUserId, status)
2) watchlistitems: (telegramUserId) and (telegramUserId, mintAddress)
3) tokens: (chain, mintAddress) unique; text-like search over symbol/name/aliases via normalized fields
4) pairs: (dex, listedAt) and (pairAddress) unique
5) raydiumscanstate: key unique

Key flows:
Command and UX principles
1) Keep commands fast and deterministic; avoid multi-step unless needed.
2) Prefer inline buttons for common actions after a /price response: Add to watchlist, Set alert above, Set alert below, View watchlist, Remove.
3) Use disambiguation when input is ambiguous: show a short inline list of matching tokens (symbol + name + partial mint).

Flow: /start
1) Register user (upsert in MongoDB) and store chatId.
2) Show a compact menu with inline buttons:
   1) Price lookup help
   2) Watchlist
   3) Alerts
   4) New listings settings

Flow: /price <symbolOrAddress>
1) Resolve input via TokenResolverService:
   1) If exact mint address: map to token.
   2) If symbol: search known tokens; if multiple results, prompt with inline disambiguation.
2) Fetch market data via MarketDataService:
   1) Use cached quote if fresh.
   2) If stale, attempt refresh with timeout.
   3) If API fails, return last known price with “data may be delayed” note; if no cache, return “API temporarily unavailable”.
3) Respond with:
   1) Current price
   2) 24h change (percentage and direction)
   3) Last updated timestamp
   4) Optional: liquidity/volume if available
4) Provide inline buttons:
   1) Add to watchlist
   2) Set alert above
   3) Set alert below
   4) View alerts for this token

Flow: Watchlist management
1) /watchlist
   1) Show watchlist items with current price snapshot (best-effort; batch fetch with caching).
   2) For each item, provide inline actions: Remove, Set alert above/below, Refresh.
2) /watchlistadd <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then add item if not present.
3) /watchlistremove <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then remove.

Flow: Alerts management
1) /alert <symbolOrAddress> <above|below> <targetPrice>
   1) Resolve token.
   2) Validate targetPrice against sane bounds.
   3) Create alert as active.
   4) Reply with summary and inline actions: Pause, Delete, Add opposite-direction alert.
2) /alerts
   1) List active alerts grouped by token; show target, direction, last evaluated, and status.
   2) Inline actions per alert: Pause/Resume, Delete.
3) /alertremove <alertId>
   1) Soft-delete or mark deleted.

Flow: Alert evaluation and notification
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Query active alerts; batch them by mintAddress.
3) For each token batch:
   1) Fetch current price once.
   2) Evaluate all alerts:
      1) Above: trigger if price >= target and alert is armed.
      2) Below: trigger if price <= target and alert is armed.
      3) Re-arming policy: optionally re-arm when price crosses back over/under a configurable hysteresis band to avoid rapid flapping.
   3) On trigger:
      1) Record trigger in alert (status triggered or keep active but disarmed).
      2) Send message with price, target, and quick actions (Delete, Set new target, View chart link if you support external links).
      3) Write notificationlog entry to dedupe.

Flow: Raydium new pair scanning
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Fetch list of recent pairs since cursor (or lastScanAt).
3) Normalize pairs:
   1) Identify base/quote mints.
   2) Store pair if new.
   3) Enrich token metadata if missing.
4) Determine which users should be notified:
   1) Users who enabled new listings.
   2) Apply filters: quote token, min liquidity, blacklist.
5) Send a compact “new listing” notification with inline actions:
   1) Price
   2) Add to watchlist
   3) Set alert

Flow: “API down” fallback behavior
1) Maintain API health state in memory (and optionally persisted counters).
2) If market data API is degraded:
   1) Prefer cached responses for /price and watchlist.
   2) Alert evaluation loop can either pause (safer) or run on cached prices with strong disclaimers; recommended: pause and notify admins via logs.
3) All user-facing errors should be short, actionable, and not expose stack traces.

Flow: Inline callback handling
1) Inline button payloads should include minimal identifiers (token mint, alertId).
2) Validate ownership: ensure the telegramUserId matches the entity being modified.
3) Always acknowledge callback quickly and then edit message or send a follow-up for longer operations.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, command routing, and inline keyboards.

Persistence
1) MongoDB (already integrated) as the primary datastore for users, tokens, pairs, watchlists, alerts, scan cursors, and notification logs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caching (market data, token resolution results, API health) to reduce latency and upstream calls.

External integrations
1) Raydium pair discovery endpoint(s) for detecting new listings.
2) Market data provider(s) for spot price and 24h change. Use at least one primary and one fallback source.
3) Optional: object storage or simple filesystem storage if you later add token logos, cached metadata, or exported reports (not required for MVP).

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) Other env vars: MongoDB connection string, polling/webhook mode selection, scan intervals, rate limits, feature flags.

Deployment
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single service process.
2) Either long polling or webhook mode depending on environment; both supported by grammY. Webhook preferred if you have stable HTTPS ingress, polling acceptable for simplicity.

Observability
1) Structured logging (request context: update id, user id, command).
2) Basic metrics counters tracked in memory and emitted to logs (API failures, alert triggers, scan cycle durations).
3) Error reporting via logs; optionally persist critical failures and last-success timestamps in MongoDB for visibility.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Data consistency: use canonical identifiers (mint/pair addresses) internally; never rely solely on symbol.
2) Idempotency: notificationlog and alert triggerState prevent duplicate alerts during retries or restarts.
3) Safe restarts: background loops read their state from MongoDB (scan cursor, active alerts) so the service can restart without losing progress.

Performance and rate limiting
1) Batch market data requests by token for watchlist and alert evaluation to minimize API calls.
2) Use caching with short TTLs (e.g., 5–30 seconds for price depending on rate limits) and separate longer TTLs for token metadata.
3) Enforce per-user command rate limits (e.g., bursts) and global upstream API rate limits.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Validate and sanitize all user inputs (numbers, symbols, addresses).
2) Ownership checks for any mutation via callbacks or commands.
3) Avoid leaking internal IDs; if you expose alertId, use short opaque IDs or MongoDB ObjectId but ensure access checks.

User experience
1) Fast responses: prefer cached prices; if fresh fetch is slow, reply with cached value and a “refreshing” follow-up only if needed.
2) Clear messaging: show timestamp of price data.
3) Inline buttons for common follow-ups to reduce typing.

Operations
1) Health signals: store lastSuccessfulScanAt and lastSuccessfulMarketFetchAt; log warnings when stale.
2) Backoff strategies: exponential backoff on failing APIs; do not hammer upstream.
3) MongoDB indexes and cleanup: TTL-like cleanup via a periodic job for old notification logs if needed.

Testing strategy (design-level)
1) Contract tests for market data and Raydium endpoints using recorded responses.
2) Simulated alert evaluation with deterministic price sequences to verify crossing logic and re-arming.
3) Load simulation for many alerts to validate batching and latency.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initialize the bot and show the main menu with quick actions. (usage: /start) [Getting started]
• /help — Show usage instructions and examples for common tasks (price, watchlist, alerts, listings). (usage: /help) [Getting started]
• /price — Lookup current price and 24h change for a token by symbol or mint address. (usage: /price <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Prices]
• /watchlist — Show your watchlist with quick actions (remove, set alerts, refresh). (usage: /watchlist) [Watchlist]
• /watchlistadd — Add a token to your watchlist by symbol or mint address. (usage: /watchlistadd <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Watchlist]
• /watchlistremove — Remove a token from your watchlist by symbol or mint address. (usage: /watchlistremove <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Watchlist]
• /alert — Create a price alert for a token above or below a target price. (usage: /alert <symbolOrMintAddress> <above|below> <targetPrice>) [Alerts]
• /alerts — List your alerts and manage them with inline actions (pause/resume/delete). (usage: /alerts) [Alerts]
• /alertremove — Remove an alert by its identifier (also available via inline buttons). (usage: /alertremove <alertId>) [Alerts]
• /listings — Show the most recently discovered Raydium listings (with filters applied if configured). (usage: /listings) [Discovery]
• /listingson — Enable new Raydium listing notifications. (usage: /listingson) [Discovery]
• /listingsoff — Disable new Raydium listing notifications. (usage: /listingsoff) [Discovery]
• /settings — View and adjust settings such as quote currency and listing filters. (usage: /settings) [Settings]

planSections.productGoals:
Primary user goals
1) Discover: Get notified when new Raydium pairs are listed (optionally filtered by criteria like liquidity, age, or quote token).
2) Track: Maintain a personal watchlist of tokens/pairs and quickly retrieve the latest price and 24h change.
3) Act: Set price alerts above/below targets per token and receive timely notifications when targets are crossed.

Product scope and assumptions
1) Token identification must be unambiguous. Symbols are not unique in crypto, so the bot should internally key on token mint address (Solana) and/or pair address, while allowing user-friendly symbol input with disambiguation.
2) Price and 24h change should come from a reliable price source. The bot should use at least one primary market data source and support a fallback source when the primary is degraded.
3) Raydium listing scanning should be robust against API changes and rate limits. The bot should avoid spamming users and provide configurable thresholds.

Success criteria
1) Alerts fire accurately with minimal false positives and no repeated spam.
2) Bot remains responsive even when upstream APIs fail (clear “API down” messages and cached values).
3) Operationally simple: single process, persistent state in MongoDB, and predictable background job behavior.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
High-level architecture (single Node.js process)
1) Telegram interface layer: grammY bot handling commands, inline button callbacks, and conversational prompts.
2) Application services layer:
   1) TokenResolverService: resolves user input (symbol, address) to a canonical token record; handles disambiguation.
   2) MarketDataService: fetches price, 24h change, liquidity/volume where available; supports caching and fallback.
   3) WatchlistService: CRUD for watchlist items per user.
   4) AlertsService: CRUD for alerts, evaluation logic, deduplication, and notification formatting.
   5) RaydiumScannerService: periodically scans for new pairs, normalizes them, and emits “new listing” events.
   6) NotificationService: sends messages to users; central place for rate limiting, retries, and formatting.
3) Persistence layer (MongoDB): users, watchlists, alerts, token metadata, pair metadata, scanner cursors/checkpoints, and notification logs.
4) In-memory caching: short-lived caches for market data, token resolution results, and API health status.

Background scheduling within the single process
1) Raydium scan loop: runs on an interval, uses a cursor/checkpoint to only process new pairs.
2) Alert evaluation loop: runs frequently, pulls active alerts, batches by token, fetches prices once per token per cycle, evaluates conditions, and triggers notifications.
3) Optional cleanup loop: expires old logs, stale cache entries, and prunes resolved-but-unused token aliases.

Resilience patterns
1) Timeouts and circuit breaker behavior per upstream API: after repeated failures, mark API as degraded for a cool-down period.
2) Stale-while-revalidate caching: serve last known price for a short window with a disclaimer if fresh fetch fails.
3) Idempotent notifications: store alert trigger state so the same crossing does not repeatedly notify.

Operational boundaries
1) No external queues: all background work occurs in-process. MongoDB is the source of truth for state and cursor checkpoints.
2) Rate limiting: apply per-user command throttles and global API call throttles to prevent abuse and upstream bans.
planSections.dataModel:
Core entities (MongoDB collections)
1) users
   1) telegramUserId (unique)
   2) chatId (for notifications; usually equals telegramUserId for private chats)
   3) createdAt, updatedAt
   4) settings
      1) quoteCurrency (e.g., USD)
      2) newListingsEnabled (boolean)
      3) newListingsFilters (minLiquidity, quoteToken, blacklists)
      4) timezone/locale (optional)

2) tokens
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) mintAddress (unique per chain)
   3) symbol, name, decimals
   4) logoUrl (optional)
   5) knownAliases (array of symbols/names for search)
   6) lastResolvedAt

3) pairs
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) dex ("raydium")
   3) pairAddress (unique)
   4) baseMintAddress, quoteMintAddress
   5) listedAt (first seen time)
   6) metricsSnapshot (liquidity, volume, price if available at discovery time)
   7) discoverySourceMetadata (raw identifiers needed for debugging)

4) watchlists
   1) telegramUserId
   2) items (or separate collection watchlistitems)
      1) type ("token" or "pair")
      2) mintAddress and/or pairAddress
      3) addedAt
   Notes: Using a separate watchlistitems collection scales better and simplifies queries.

5) alerts
   1) telegramUserId
   2) chain
   3) mintAddress (or pairAddress if you want pair-specific pricing)
   4) direction ("above" | "below")
   5) targetPrice (in user quote currency)
   6) status ("active" | "paused" | "triggered" | "deleted")
   7) createdAt, updatedAt
   8) lastEvaluatedAt
   9) lastNotifiedAt
   10) triggerState
      1) lastPriceSeen
      2) armed (boolean) to prevent repeated triggers without recross logic

6) raydiumscanstate
   1) key (e.g., "raydiumpairs")
   2) cursor (block/slot/time/page token depending on API)
   3) lastScanAt
   4) lastSuccessAt
   5) consecutiveFailures

7) notificationlog (optional but recommended)
   1) telegramUserId
   2) type ("alerttrigger" | "newlisting")
   3) entityId (alertId or pairAddress)
   4) sentAt
   5) messageHash or dedupeKey
   Purpose: diagnose delivery issues and prevent duplicates.

Indexes
1) alerts: (status, chain, mintAddress) and (telegramUserId, status)
2) watchlistitems: (telegramUserId) and (telegramUserId, mintAddress)
3) tokens: (chain, mintAddress) unique; text-like search over symbol/name/aliases via normalized fields
4) pairs: (dex, listedAt) and (pairAddress) unique
5) raydiumscanstate: key unique
planSections.keyFlows:
Command and UX principles
1) Keep commands fast and deterministic; avoid multi-step unless needed.
2) Prefer inline buttons for common actions after a /price response: Add to watchlist, Set alert above, Set alert below, View watchlist, Remove.
3) Use disambiguation when input is ambiguous: show a short inline list of matching tokens (symbol + name + partial mint).

Flow: /start
1) Register user (upsert in MongoDB) and store chatId.
2) Show a compact menu with inline buttons:
   1) Price lookup help
   2) Watchlist
   3) Alerts
   4) New listings settings

Flow: /price <symbolOrAddress>
1) Resolve input via TokenResolverService:
   1) If exact mint address: map to token.
   2) If symbol: search known tokens; if multiple results, prompt with inline disambiguation.
2) Fetch market data via MarketDataService:
   1) Use cached quote if fresh.
   2) If stale, attempt refresh with timeout.
   3) If API fails, return last known price with “data may be delayed” note; if no cache, return “API temporarily unavailable”.
3) Respond with:
   1) Current price
   2) 24h change (percentage and direction)
   3) Last updated timestamp
   4) Optional: liquidity/volume if available
4) Provide inline buttons:
   1) Add to watchlist
   2) Set alert above
   3) Set alert below
   4) View alerts for this token

Flow: Watchlist management
1) /watchlist
   1) Show watchlist items with current price snapshot (best-effort; batch fetch with caching).
   2) For each item, provide inline actions: Remove, Set alert above/below, Refresh.
2) /watchlistadd <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then add item if not present.
3) /watchlistremove <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then remove.

Flow: Alerts management
1) /alert <symbolOrAddress> <above|below> <targetPrice>
   1) Resolve token.
   2) Validate targetPrice against sane bounds.
   3) Create alert as active.
   4) Reply with summary and inline actions: Pause, Delete, Add opposite-direction alert.
2) /alerts
   1) List active alerts grouped by token; show target, direction, last evaluated, and status.
   2) Inline actions per alert: Pause/Resume, Delete.
3) /alertremove <alertId>
   1) Soft-delete or mark deleted.

Flow: Alert evaluation and notification
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Query active alerts; batch them by mintAddress.
3) For each token batch:
   1) Fetch current price once.
   2) Evaluate all alerts:
      1) Above: trigger if price >= target and alert is armed.
      2) Below: trigger if price <= target and alert is armed.
      3) Re-arming policy: optionally re-arm when price crosses back over/under a configurable hysteresis band to avoid rapid flapping.
   3) On trigger:
      1) Record trigger in alert (status triggered or keep active but disarmed).
      2) Send message with price, target, and quick actions (Delete, Set new target, View chart link if you support external links).
      3) Write notificationlog entry to dedupe.

Flow: Raydium new pair scanning
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Fetch list of recent pairs since cursor (or lastScanAt).
3) Normalize pairs:
   1) Identify base/quote mints.
   2) Store pair if new.
   3) Enrich token metadata if missing.
4) Determine which users should be notified:
   1) Users who enabled new listings.
   2) Apply filters: quote token, min liquidity, blacklist.
5) Send a compact “new listing” notification with inline actions:
   1) Price
   2) Add to watchlist
   3) Set alert

Flow: “API down” fallback behavior
1) Maintain API health state in memory (and optionally persisted counters).
2) If market data API is degraded:
   1) Prefer cached responses for /price and watchlist.
   2) Alert evaluation loop can either pause (safer) or run on cached prices with strong disclaimers; recommended: pause and notify admins via logs.
3) All user-facing errors should be short, actionable, and not expose stack traces.

Flow: Inline callback handling
1) Inline button payloads should include minimal identifiers (token mint, alertId).
2) Validate ownership: ensure the telegramUserId matches the entity being modified.
3) Always acknowledge callback quickly and then edit message or send a follow-up for longer operations.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, command routing, and inline keyboards.

Persistence
1) MongoDB (already integrated) as the primary datastore for users, tokens, pairs, watchlists, alerts, scan cursors, and notification logs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caching (market data, token resolution results, API health) to reduce latency and upstream calls.

External integrations
1) Raydium pair discovery endpoint(s) for detecting new listings.
2) Market data provider(s) for spot price and 24h change. Use at least one primary and one fallback source.
3) Optional: object storage or simple filesystem storage if you later add token logos, cached metadata, or exported reports (not required for MVP).

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) Other env vars: MongoDB connection string, polling/webhook mode selection, scan intervals, rate limits, feature flags.

Deployment
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single service process.
2) Either long polling or webhook mode depending on environment; both supported by grammY. Webhook preferred if you have stable HTTPS ingress, polling acceptable for simplicity.

Observability
1) Structured logging (request context: update id, user id, command).
2) Basic metrics counters tracked in memory and emitted to logs (API failures, alert triggers, scan cycle durations).
3) Error reporting via logs; optionally persist critical failures and last-success timestamps in MongoDB for visibility.

DOCS.md.content:
Solana Alert Watcher

What this bot does
It helps you look up Solana token prices, keep a personal watchlist, set one-time price alerts (above or below), and optionally get notified when new Raydium pairs are discovered.

Public commands
1) /start
Shows a welcome message and a menu.

2) /help
Shows usage examples and explains behavior when external APIs are unavailable.

3) /price <symbol|mint>
Looks up a token and shows its price in USD.
Examples:
/price SOL
/price So11111111111111111111111111111111111111112
Notes:
If you use a symbol and it matches multiple tokens, the bot may ask you to pick one by mint.

4) /watchlist
Opens the watchlist menu.
Subcommands:
/watchlist list
/watchlist add <symbol|mint>
/watchlist remove <symbol|mint>

5) /alerts
Opens the alerts menu and lists current alerts.

6) /alert <symbol|mint> above <price>
Creates an above alert.
Example:
/alert SOL above 250

7) /alert <symbol|mint> below <price>
Creates a below alert.
Example:
/alert SOL below 100

8) /reset
Clears your watchlist and alerts for this bot.

Inline buttons
After a /price lookup, you will see buttons like:
- Add to Watchlist
- Set Alert Above
- Set Alert Below
- Refresh

New pairs notifications
New Raydium pair notifications are opt-in.
Use the New Pairs menu from /start to enable or disable.

Environment variables
Required
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Your Telegram bot token.

Optional (recommended)
2) MONGODB_URI
MongoDB connection string. When set, your watchlist, alerts, and settings are persisted.
If missing, the bot still runs but data is stored in memory and is lost on restart.

Optional
3) PRICE_API_BASE_URL
Override the price API base URL. If not set, the bot uses a safe default.

4) RAYDIUM_API_BASE_URL
Override the Raydium API base URL. If not set, the bot uses a safe default.

5) CACHE_TTL_SECONDS
TTL for in-memory caches. Default is used if missing.

6) ALERT_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS
Alert scanner interval. Default is used if missing.

7) RAYDIUM_SCAN_INTERVAL_SECONDS
Raydium new pairs scanner interval. Default is used if missing.

Troubleshooting
If a price or Raydium call fails, the bot will say:
“Price data temporarily unavailable (API down). Please try again in a minute.”
It will keep running and retry later.

README.md.content:
Solana Alert Watcher

This is a Telegram bot (grammY, Node.js ES modules) that provides Solana token price lookup, per-user watchlists, per-user price alerts (above/below), and background scanning of newly listed Raydium pairs.

Features
1) /price lookup by symbol or mint
2) Watchlist add/remove/list (per user)
3) Alerts above/below (per user, trigger once)
4) Raydium new pair scanning (background) with opt-in notifications
5) Caching, timeouts, and friendly “API temporarily unavailable” messaging
6) MongoDB persistence (watchlists, alerts, users, Raydium state)

Architecture overview
1) src/index.js
   Starts the bot with long polling via @grammyjs/runner, clears webhook, and starts background pollers.
2) src/bot.js
   Creates the grammY bot, registers commands first, then callback handlers.
3) src/commands/*
   /start, /help, /price, /watchlist, /alerts, /alert, /reset
4) src/services/*
   priceService (token resolution + price), raydiumService (new pairs scan)
5) src/pollers/*
   alertPoller, raydiumScanner (single-process loops)
6) src/lib/*
   config, db, caches, logging, helpers

Setup
1) Install
   npm run install:root

2) Configure env
   Copy .env.sample to .env and set:
   - TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
   - MONGODB_URI (optional but strongly recommended; required for persistence)

3) Run locally
   npm run dev

4) Run production
   npm start

Commands (quick)
1) /start
2) /help
3) /price SOL
4) /price <mint>
5) /watchlist
6) /watchlist add SOL
7) /watchlist remove <mint>
8) /watchlist list
9) /alerts
10) /alert SOL above 250
11) /alert SOL below 100
12) /reset (clears your stored conversation memory collection if you had it; this bot uses /reset to clear watchlist+alerts state for safety)

Integrations
1) Price API
   - Configurable via PRICE_API_BASE_URL (optional)
   - Default fallback uses Jupiter public APIs
2) Raydium API
   - Configurable via RAYDIUM_API_BASE_URL (optional)
   - Default fallback uses Raydium public APIs

Database
Collections used
1) users
2) watchlists
3) alerts
4) raydium_state
5) raydium_pairs

Indexes are created on boot when MongoDB is configured.

Deployment notes (Render)
1) Use a Background Worker or Web Service. Long polling does not require an inbound port.
2) Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
3) Set MONGODB_URI to enable persistence.

Troubleshooting
1) Bot not starting
   - Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set
   - Check logs for "TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN set: true"
2) Price data unavailable
   - Upstream APIs can be down or rate-limited. The bot will return a friendly message and keep running.
3) Duplicate instance conflict (409)
   - Render deploy overlaps can cause 409 conflicts. This bot retries polling with backoff.

Extensibility
Add new commands under src/commands and they will be auto-registered via src/commands/loader.js. Keep /help output in sync.

aiMessages.content:
Build a Telegram crypto price + alerts bot:

- scan new pair of solana listing on raydium
- Watchlist: add/remove coins and list watchlist.
- Price lookup: /price <symbol> (returns price + 24h change).
- Alerts: set above/below alerts per coin; list and remove alerts.
- Notifications: alert users when targets are hit.
- Reliability: caching, graceful errors, and “API down” fallback message.
- UX: quick inline buttons for common actions (Add alert, Remove, Watchlist).

Created the Solana Alert Watcher Telegram bot project with grammY, MongoDB persistence (with in-memory fallback), /start /help /price /watchlist /alerts /alert /reset, inline button flows, TTL caching, and two in-process background pollers (alerts + Raydium scanner) with backoff and production-safe logs.

Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Crypto Price + Alerts Bot (Solana/Raydium listings)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot built with Node.js (ESM) and grammY that helps users discover newly listed Solana pairs on Raydium, track prices, manage a personal watchlist, and receive above/below price alerts via Telegram notifications.

The bot prioritizes reliability (caching, timeouts, graceful degradation when upstream APIs are unavailable), good UX (inline buttons and guided flows), and operational simplicity (single Node.js service process with MongoDB for persistence). It will continuously scan Raydium for new listings, normalize tokens/pairs, and provide both user-driven lookups (/price) and event-driven notifications (new listings, alert triggers).

Product goals:
Primary user goals
1) Discover: Get notified when new Raydium pairs are listed (optionally filtered by criteria like liquidity, age, or quote token).
2) Track: Maintain a personal watchlist of tokens/pairs and quickly retrieve the latest price and 24h change.
3) Act: Set price alerts above/below targets per token and receive timely notifications when targets are crossed.

Product scope and assumptions
1) Token identification must be unambiguous. Symbols are not unique in crypto, so the bot should internally key on token mint address (Solana) and/or pair address, while allowing user-friendly symbol input with disambiguation.
2) Price and 24h change should come from a reliable price source. The bot should use at least one primary market data source and support a fallback source when the primary is degraded.
3) Raydium listing scanning should be robust against API changes and rate limits. The bot should avoid spamming users and provide configurable thresholds.

Success criteria
1) Alerts fire accurately with minimal false positives and no repeated spam.
2) Bot remains responsive even when upstream APIs fail (clear “API down” messages and cached values).
3) Operationally simple: single process, persistent state in MongoDB, and predictable background job behavior.

High-level architecture:
High-level architecture (single Node.js process)
1) Telegram interface layer: grammY bot handling commands, inline button callbacks, and conversational prompts.
2) Application services layer:
   1) TokenResolverService: resolves user input (symbol, address) to a canonical token record; handles disambiguation.
   2) MarketDataService: fetches price, 24h change, liquidity/volume where available; supports caching and fallback.
   3) WatchlistService: CRUD for watchlist items per user.
   4) AlertsService: CRUD for alerts, evaluation logic, deduplication, and notification formatting.
   5) RaydiumScannerService: periodically scans for new pairs, normalizes them, and emits “new listing” events.
   6) NotificationService: sends messages to users; central place for rate limiting, retries, and formatting.
3) Persistence layer (MongoDB): users, watchlists, alerts, token metadata, pair metadata, scanner cursors/checkpoints, and notification logs.
4) In-memory caching: short-lived caches for market data, token resolution results, and API health status.

Background scheduling within the single process
1) Raydium scan loop: runs on an interval, uses a cursor/checkpoint to only process new pairs.
2) Alert evaluation loop: runs frequently, pulls active alerts, batches by token, fetches prices once per token per cycle, evaluates conditions, and triggers notifications.
3) Optional cleanup loop: expires old logs, stale cache entries, and prunes resolved-but-unused token aliases.

Resilience patterns
1) Timeouts and circuit breaker behavior per upstream API: after repeated failures, mark API as degraded for a cool-down period.
2) Stale-while-revalidate caching: serve last known price for a short window with a disclaimer if fresh fetch fails.
3) Idempotent notifications: store alert trigger state so the same crossing does not repeatedly notify.

Operational boundaries
1) No external queues: all background work occurs in-process. MongoDB is the source of truth for state and cursor checkpoints.
2) Rate limiting: apply per-user command throttles and global API call throttles to prevent abuse and upstream bans.

Data model:
Core entities (MongoDB collections)
1) users
   1) telegramUserId (unique)
   2) chatId (for notifications; usually equals telegramUserId for private chats)
   3) createdAt, updatedAt
   4) settings
      1) quoteCurrency (e.g., USD)
      2) newListingsEnabled (boolean)
      3) newListingsFilters (minLiquidity, quoteToken, blacklists)
      4) timezone/locale (optional)

2) tokens
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) mintAddress (unique per chain)
   3) symbol, name, decimals
   4) logoUrl (optional)
   5) knownAliases (array of symbols/names for search)
   6) lastResolvedAt

3) pairs
   1) chain ("solana")
   2) dex ("raydium")
   3) pairAddress (unique)
   4) baseMintAddress, quoteMintAddress
   5) listedAt (first seen time)
   6) metricsSnapshot (liquidity, volume, price if available at discovery time)
   7) discoverySourceMetadata (raw identifiers needed for debugging)

4) watchlists
   1) telegramUserId
   2) items (or separate collection watchlistitems)
      1) type ("token" or "pair")
      2) mintAddress and/or pairAddress
      3) addedAt
   Notes: Using a separate watchlistitems collection scales better and simplifies queries.

5) alerts
   1) telegramUserId
   2) chain
   3) mintAddress (or pairAddress if you want pair-specific pricing)
   4) direction ("above" | "below")
   5) targetPrice (in user quote currency)
   6) status ("active" | "paused" | "triggered" | "deleted")
   7) createdAt, updatedAt
   8) lastEvaluatedAt
   9) lastNotifiedAt
   10) triggerState
      1) lastPriceSeen
      2) armed (boolean) to prevent repeated triggers without recross logic

6) raydiumscanstate
   1) key (e.g., "raydiumpairs")
   2) cursor (block/slot/time/page token depending on API)
   3) lastScanAt
   4) lastSuccessAt
   5) consecutiveFailures

7) notificationlog (optional but recommended)
   1) telegramUserId
   2) type ("alerttrigger" | "newlisting")
   3) entityId (alertId or pairAddress)
   4) sentAt
   5) messageHash or dedupeKey
   Purpose: diagnose delivery issues and prevent duplicates.

Indexes
1) alerts: (status, chain, mintAddress) and (telegramUserId, status)
2) watchlistitems: (telegramUserId) and (telegramUserId, mintAddress)
3) tokens: (chain, mintAddress) unique; text-like search over symbol/name/aliases via normalized fields
4) pairs: (dex, listedAt) and (pairAddress) unique
5) raydiumscanstate: key unique

Key flows:
Command and UX principles
1) Keep commands fast and deterministic; avoid multi-step unless needed.
2) Prefer inline buttons for common actions after a /price response: Add to watchlist, Set alert above, Set alert below, View watchlist, Remove.
3) Use disambiguation when input is ambiguous: show a short inline list of matching tokens (symbol + name + partial mint).

Flow: /start
1) Register user (upsert in MongoDB) and store chatId.
2) Show a compact menu with inline buttons:
   1) Price lookup help
   2) Watchlist
   3) Alerts
   4) New listings settings

Flow: /price <symbolOrAddress>
1) Resolve input via TokenResolverService:
   1) If exact mint address: map to token.
   2) If symbol: search known tokens; if multiple results, prompt with inline disambiguation.
2) Fetch market data via MarketDataService:
   1) Use cached quote if fresh.
   2) If stale, attempt refresh with timeout.
   3) If API fails, return last known price with “data may be delayed” note; if no cache, return “API temporarily unavailable”.
3) Respond with:
   1) Current price
   2) 24h change (percentage and direction)
   3) Last updated timestamp
   4) Optional: liquidity/volume if available
4) Provide inline buttons:
   1) Add to watchlist
   2) Set alert above
   3) Set alert below
   4) View alerts for this token

Flow: Watchlist management
1) /watchlist
   1) Show watchlist items with current price snapshot (best-effort; batch fetch with caching).
   2) For each item, provide inline actions: Remove, Set alert above/below, Refresh.
2) /watchlistadd <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then add item if not present.
3) /watchlistremove <symbolOrAddress>
   1) Resolve token, then remove.

Flow: Alerts management
1) /alert <symbolOrAddress> <above|below> <targetPrice>
   1) Resolve token.
   2) Validate targetPrice against sane bounds.
   3) Create alert as active.
   4) Reply with summary and inline actions: Pause, Delete, Add opposite-direction alert.
2) /alerts
   1) List active alerts grouped by token; show target, direction, last evaluated, and status.
   2) Inline actions per alert: Pause/Resume, Delete.
3) /alertremove <alertId>
   1) Soft-delete or mark deleted.

Flow: Alert evaluation and notification
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Query active alerts; batch them by mintAddress.
3) For each token batch:
   1) Fetch current price once.
   2) Evaluate all alerts:
      1) Above: trigger if price >= target and alert is armed.
      2) Below: trigger if price <= target and alert is armed.
      3) Re-arming policy: optionally re-arm when price crosses back over/under a configurable hysteresis band to avoid rapid flapping.
   3) On trigger:
      1) Record trigger in alert (status triggered or keep active but disarmed).
      2) Send message with price, target, and quick actions (Delete, Set new target, View chart link if you support external links).
      3) Write notificationlog entry to dedupe.

Flow: Raydium new pair scanning
1) Scheduler wakes on interval.
2) Fetch list of recent pairs since cursor (or lastScanAt).
3) Normalize pairs:
   1) Identify base/quote mints.
   2) Store pair if new.
   3) Enrich token metadata if missing.
4) Determine which users should be notified:
   1) Users who enabled new listings.
   2) Apply filters: quote token, min liquidity, blacklist.
5) Send a compact “new listing” notification with inline actions:
   1) Price
   2) Add to watchlist
   3) Set alert

Flow: “API down” fallback behavior
1) Maintain API health state in memory (and optionally persisted counters).
2) If market data API is degraded:
   1) Prefer cached responses for /price and watchlist.
   2) Alert evaluation loop can either pause (safer) or run on cached prices with strong disclaimers; recommended: pause and notify admins via logs.
3) All user-facing errors should be short, actionable, and not expose stack traces.

Flow: Inline callback handling
1) Inline button payloads should include minimal identifiers (token mint, alertId).
2) Validate ownership: ensure the telegramUserId matches the entity being modified.
3) Always acknowledge callback quickly and then edit message or send a follow-up for longer operations.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, command routing, and inline keyboards.

Persistence
1) MongoDB (already integrated) as the primary datastore for users, tokens, pairs, watchlists, alerts, scan cursors, and notification logs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caching (market data, token resolution results, API health) to reduce latency and upstream calls.

External integrations
1) Raydium pair discovery endpoint(s) for detecting new listings.
2) Market data provider(s) for spot price and 24h change. Use at least one primary and one fallback source.
3) Optional: object storage or simple filesystem storage if you later add token logos, cached metadata, or exported reports (not required for MVP).

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) Other env vars: MongoDB connection string, polling/webhook mode selection, scan intervals, rate limits, feature flags.

Deployment
1) A Node.js server in the cloud running the single service process.
2) Either long polling or webhook mode depending on environment; both supported by grammY. Webhook preferred if you have stable HTTPS ingress, polling acceptable for simplicity.

Observability
1) Structured logging (request context: update id, user id, command).
2) Basic metrics counters tracked in memory and emitted to logs (API failures, alert triggers, scan cycle durations).
3) Error reporting via logs; optionally persist critical failures and last-success timestamps in MongoDB for visibility.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Data consistency: use canonical identifiers (mint/pair addresses) internally; never rely solely on symbol.
2) Idempotency: notificationlog and alert triggerState prevent duplicate alerts during retries or restarts.
3) Safe restarts: background loops read their state from MongoDB (scan cursor, active alerts) so the service can restart without losing progress.

Performance and rate limiting
1) Batch market data requests by token for watchlist and alert evaluation to minimize API calls.
2) Use caching with short TTLs (e.g., 5–30 seconds for price depending on rate limits) and separate longer TTLs for token metadata.
3) Enforce per-user command rate limits (e.g., bursts) and global upstream API rate limits.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Validate and sanitize all user inputs (numbers, symbols, addresses).
2) Ownership checks for any mutation via callbacks or commands.
3) Avoid leaking internal IDs; if you expose alertId, use short opaque IDs or MongoDB ObjectId but ensure access checks.

User experience
1) Fast responses: prefer cached prices; if fresh fetch is slow, reply with cached value and a “refreshing” follow-up only if needed.
2) Clear messaging: show timestamp of price data.
3) Inline buttons for common follow-ups to reduce typing.

Operations
1) Health signals: store lastSuccessfulScanAt and lastSuccessfulMarketFetchAt; log warnings when stale.
2) Backoff strategies: exponential backoff on failing APIs; do not hammer upstream.
3) MongoDB indexes and cleanup: TTL-like cleanup via a periodic job for old notification logs if needed.

Testing strategy (design-level)
1) Contract tests for market data and Raydium endpoints using recorded responses.
2) Simulated alert evaluation with deterministic price sequences to verify crossing logic and re-arming.
3) Load simulation for many alerts to validate batching and latency.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initialize the bot and show the main menu with quick actions. (usage: /start) [Getting started]
• /help — Show usage instructions and examples for common tasks (price, watchlist, alerts, listings). (usage: /help) [Getting started]
• /price — Lookup current price and 24h change for a token by symbol or mint address. (usage: /price <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Prices]
• /watchlist — Show your watchlist with quick actions (remove, set alerts, refresh). (usage: /watchlist) [Watchlist]
• /watchlistadd — Add a token to your watchlist by symbol or mint address. (usage: /watchlistadd <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Watchlist]
• /watchlistremove — Remove a token from your watchlist by symbol or mint address. (usage: /watchlistremove <symbolOrMintAddress>) [Watchlist]
• /alert — Create a price alert for a token above or below a target price. (usage: /alert <symbolOrMintAddress> <above|below> <targetPrice>) [Alerts]
• /alerts — List your alerts and manage them with inline actions (pause/resume/delete). (usage: /alerts) [Alerts]
• /alertremove — Remove an alert by its identifier (also available via inline buttons). (usage: /alertremove <alertId>) [Alerts]
• /listings — Show the most recently discovered Raydium listings (with filters applied if configured). (usage: /listings) [Discovery]
• /listingson — Enable new Raydium listing notifications. (usage: /listingson) [Discovery]
• /listingsoff — Disable new Raydium listing notifications. (usage: /listingsoff) [Discovery]
• /settings — View and adjust settings such as quote currency and listing filters. (usage: /settings) [Settings]

Performance and rate limiting
1) Batch market data requests by token for watchlist and alert evaluation to minimize API calls.
2) Use caching with short TTLs (e.g., 5–30 seconds for price depending on rate limits) and separate longer TTLs for token metadata.
3) Enforce per-user command rate limits (e.g., bursts) and global upstream API rate limits.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Validate and sanitize all user inputs (numbers, symbols, addresses).
2) Ownership checks for any mutation via callbacks or commands.
3) Avoid leaking internal IDs; if you expose alertId, use short opaque IDs or MongoDB ObjectId but ensure access checks.

User experience
1) Fast responses: prefer cached prices; if fresh fetch is slow, reply with cached value and a “refreshing” follow-up only if needed.
2) Clear messaging: show timestamp of price data.
3) Inline buttons for common follow-ups to reduce typing.

Operations
1) Health signals: store lastSuccessfulScanAt and lastSuccessfulMarketFetchAt; log warnings when stale.
2) Backoff strategies: exponential backoff on failing APIs; do not hammer upstream.
3) MongoDB indexes and cleanup: TTL-like cleanup via a periodic job for old notification logs if needed.

Testing strategy (design-level)
1) Contract tests for market data and Raydium endpoints using recorded responses.
2) Simulated alert evaluation with deterministic price sequences to verify crossing logic and re-arming.
3) Load simulation for many alerts to validate batching and latency.

envVarDocs.title: MongoDB Connection String
envVarDocs.description: Optional but strongly recommended. Enables persistence for users, watchlists, alerts, and Raydium scan state.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Use MongoDB Atlas or any MongoDB provider and copy the connection string.

envVarDocs.title: Price API Base URL
envVarDocs.description: Optional override for the price API. If unset, defaults to Jupiter public API base.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by src/services/priceService.js for /price and alert polling.

envVarDocs.title: Raydium API Base URL
envVarDocs.description: Optional override for Raydium scanning API. If unset, defaults to a public Raydium API base.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by src/services/raydiumService.js for new pair scanning.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Leave empty to use defaults, or set to your preferred compatible endpoint.

envVarDocs.title: Cache TTL Seconds
envVarDocs.description: Optional. TTL for in-memory caches (token resolution and Raydium fetch).

envVarDocs.description: Optional. How often the bot checks active alerts.

envVarDocs.title: Raydium Scan Interval Seconds
